Prepared For Veronika Dimitrov

    New World Kids

    Veronika Dimitrov

    A Strategic Briefing
    07/05/26
    Begin Reading
    Part One

    Questions & Answers

    Hello Veronika, thank you for your patience. Here are the responses to the previous questions from your most recent emails and WhatsApp messages. I welcome your perspective on each response.

    Progress Report: How would you describe the progress achieved over the last two years?
    I had to shift a few things to find a new way to make money without getting stuck in the "rat race" or feeling like somebody's robot. The goal was to learn a new skill, tend to the food forest, and just hold on until I can figure things out. When I came back from the States, I chose to bet on myself as usual. I invested in a Starlink to have real internet here and studied AI and agentic engineering every day on my own. No school, no courses, just YouTube and a laptop my friend gave me. Along the way I realized that this technology could potentially be a way to solve huge problems. Not only for myself, but for others, so I doubled down on that. I began building small systems, agents, and websites for friends. I built for free at first and then very cheaply to pay the bills, improve my skills, and get real feedback. Now I'm ready to see if that bet paid off. The plan now is to publicly offer these systems to nonprofits, social-purpose companies, and people who can use them to create good in the world and increase their impact.
    Hiring Context: Why did you hire me two years ago if the primary focus at that stage was just 'letting the garden mature'?
    Good question. I hired you at that time because I don't know what I don't know. I was very new to anything to do with raising money or even knowing how to go about starting that process. I usually have a decent sense of people's intentions. After speaking with you, I felt you were trustworthy and could help somehow. You come from that world and have a lot of experience, but in the end it was more of a feeling that it was the right thing to do. As far as letting the garden mature, I knew when starting this how long it could take on the growing side. I wanted to see what else was possible on the business and financial side of this during the process. Unfortunately, personal situations and challenges arose and I had to go home, try to work and reset. I managed to do that, but it did take away my ability to focus on that side of things.
    Transparency Gap: Why is the progression currently unclear to an observer?
    On the side of seeing an active funded project, with real students coming and going or volunteers involved, it's because it doesn't exist. It's just me here. I do spend a lot of my own money trying but it seems like by the time I fix one thing, two more things always break. That's been the norm in this project: get up, get knocked down. Two steps forward, one step back. No excuses, but that's how it feels. I do get discouraged at times, but I just keep going and try to find a way.

    The growing side of the project is called "Syntropic Agriculture" and it's doing fine. It's not a typical garden project and can be quite difficult to explain or see what's happening. The typical timeline with no setbacks is 3 years to even see a difference.

    0 to 1.5 Years (Placenta Phase): Rapid soil cover and early biomass production.
    1.5 to 3 Years (Secondary Phase): Pioneer trees mature, and transitional crops like bananas and pineapples begin fruiting.
    3 to 15 Years: Canopy formation occurs, supporting long-term shrubs and fruit trees (e.g., citrus or coffee).
    15+ Years (Climax/Abundance): The mature ecosystem reaches deep nutrient cycling, yielding high-value timber alongside sustained crop harvests.

    We are in the three-year stage and I can walk through here and see it very clearly. What began as small bushes and a few trees is now huge clumps of bamboo, at least 200 different varieties of small plants, orchids, air plants, fruit trees, cacti, aloe, agave, bananas, mangoes, limes, guavas, and more. It's messy, but it's clearly working when I walk around.

    If you want to go deeper when time allows, the video below is a great example of how these systems compound over the years:
    https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LeoD1CmVj40
    Milestone Planning: What are your specific, concrete goals for 1, 3, and 5 years from now that bridge the gap from where you are today to your long-term vision?
    Year 1
    July 2026 – July 2027
    Year 3
    July 2028 – July 2029
    Year 5
    July 2030 – July 2031
    Prove that the self-funding concepts actually work and can generate income for myself and the project. Without that, it's the same old story. Nothing will change. Also, I will exhaust and contact every resource I listed in the resources section at the end of this document, and track the responses.
    If Year 1 gets traction, the next step is approaching the owners about buying this property so it can always be a school. If so, we stay. If not, I've quietly built safeguards. Everything here can be extracted and moved — trees are in buried containers, most structures are built like movie props, and furniture and domes pack flat. It moves like a circus or a Super Bowl halftime show. We'd like to stay, but we're ready either way. If this becomes possible, we get the property certified as an official permaculture site and butterfly garden. That opens many doors we can touch on later as well.
    The long-term vision is to collaborate with governments and some of the larger colleges to build a reputation and increase our influence. After five years of Syntropic Agriculture, the system doesn't need to be explained anymore. Anyone who walks through it can immediately see the potential. With the right ambience, it can serve as a beautiful place to conduct interviews and invite local officials and people who care about these types of initiatives. Governments and political people always want a quick win, and with the proper alignment, many things become possible. The sky is the limit, but now we are just barely getting out of the trenches. One step at a time is how we are playing it.
    Scaling Vision: What does 'taking this model elsewhere' look like in practice?
    Scaling this goes in two directions to accomplish the same goal: creating life skills and environments that allow youth to explore their potential. In the inner city, sometimes hunger is not the most immediate challenge. We need to understand who we serve and be realistic about what really resonates with them. We already have a group of mentors motivated to get involved and would like to further our concept of the Culture Shock Program. This would match at-risk youth from all cultures with mentors and positive activities based around art, sports, and music. We would continue our food program as an option for those who are interested and look for land grants to create a space large enough not only to grow food, but to serve as our own private lot to host any of the other programs we extend to.

    The Indigo Azul food forest in Mexico will scale in the other direction, focusing on the needs of the youth there and connecting with governments, schools, and local farmers to share ideas and increase knowledge of sustainable food systems. Ideally this would open the doors to a cultural exchange program allowing kids from here to visit my city as a group and vice versa. I think that exposure to different environments is not only healthy but can be eye-opening and motivational. Many things are possible once the foundation is solid and we can secure a longer lease here or see where we can operate long-term.
    Income Velocity: Is there a faster way for you to make money online without starting a business for it?
    Short answer: yes, of course. There is no shortage of companies that want to hire people who can use AI to help them scale. It's a great time if someone wants to go that route.

    Personal answer: I'm 47 now and 48 this year. My time is all I have and I don't feel aligned with spending it using my skills to help build and grow a corporation whose primary goal is maximizing profits at any cost and views most of their employees as expendable and replaceable. That fact is part of the reason I decided to do this project in the first place and not deal with being in the rat race or pursuing a traditional path. This is more a personal view and not a judgment. What others see as an opportunity I may see as a trap according to my experiences and perspective. On a positive note, yesterday I met with my first potential client to see if I could get signal or if all of this is just noise. They seemed very interested, really liked the preview, and will let me know how they want to proceed. They are a new nonprofit as well. The biggest takeaway from that meeting was how I felt when I hung up the phone. I was genuinely happy. I really felt good having a skill that can help people, being able to answer questions honestly and not just trying to persuade someone to “buy my thing.” I felt inspired by what they were doing and I feel this path is something positive and easy for me to feel good about at the end of the day. That means a lot to me and has a big influence on what financial paths I choose at this phase of my life. Of course, feeling good about what we do in the world doesn't usually pay the bills. The next few months will show whether I found an exception to that or not. I'll update you when I have a response. I included a chapter in the back with examples of the offers we are making and previous projects I built and sold.

    UPDATE — 7/11/26: Great news! The Asc3nd Collective closed with us today! First real signal, so I'm excited!
    Section 1

    The Framework

    Most nonprofits survive on donations, hope, and a lot of volunteer sacrifice. Most AI companies build for enterprises with deep pockets. Nobody is building AI for the organizations that need it most — the nonprofits and social-purpose businesses doing real work on the ground, with no budget and no technical team.

    That gap is where The Pauli Effect lives. We are not a website shop. We are not a SaaS subscription. We are a Seattle-based AI studio that builds autonomous systems for organizations that can't afford to wait.

    Section 2

    The Story

    Told through two entities, one engine.

    New World Kids is our proof. A 501(c)(3) nonprofit running a food forest in Puerto Vallarta and youth programs in Seattle. Five years in the dirt. 200+ plant varieties. Real land, real people, and the potential to become something impactful. It's also where our AI systems were built, tested, and proven under real conditions — not in a demo lab.

    The Pauli Effect is the studio. A licensed social-purpose company that takes the same AI systems — agents that hunt grants, post bilingual content, manage compliance, and show their work publicly — and deploys them for other nonprofits and mission-driven businesses. Clients own the system outright. One build. No lock-in. No subscription trap.

    One engine. Two faces. NWKids proves it works. The Pauli Effect sells it.

    Section 3

    What Makes This Different

    Three things most competitors won't offer.

    1. You own it.
    Not a license. Not a plan. Not a SaaS. The code, the data, the brand, the digital mascot. When we're done, it's yours — run it, modify it, or hand it to another team. Like buying a car, not leasing one. The only subscription is optional: our management package and the Knowledge API that keeps the agent current. Cancel anytime, keep everything you built. Payment plans available.

    2. It runs on any AI.
    Built on the Interpretable Context Methodology — a simple folder-based architecture, not a proprietary framework. Swap Claude for ChatGPT, or run free open-source models. When OpenAI changes their prices tomorrow, your system doesn't break. You are protected from model shift — the single biggest risk in enterprise AI.

    Your system, your data, your mission. Sovereign and Safe.

    3. It's observable.
    Every action the agent takes — every grant searched, every post drafted, every decision made — is logged to a public ledger page we build for you. Donors, boards, and funders can see the work happening in real time. Observable agents solve the trust problem that keeps nonprofits away from AI.

    Section 4

    The Big Win

    What an investor or funder is actually backing.

    The Build

    A complete AI agent system, custom-branded, deployed in 48 hours

    For nonprofits and social-purpose companies — replacing a $60K/year operations hire for a one-time build fee.

    The Knowledge API

    Weekly intelligence on grants, compliance, AI developments

    The recurring revenue layer. The agent gets smarter every week; the subscription funds the mission.

    The Proof Site

    NWKids, running live

    A real, public, verifiable case study. Not a pitch deck — a URL.

    The Moat

    Domain-specific agents for nonprofits — a vertical nobody owns yet

    Domain-specific agents are 80%+ more token-efficient and don't really exist yet. We intend to be one of the first movers in our state in that space.

    Section 5

    The Offer

    Structured. Simple. Sustainable.

    Outcome: A digital employee that runs your nonprofit's fundraising, compliance, and content — owned forever.

    Mechanism: Hermes open-source agents that have their own computer in the cloud and can take action for your mission. Websites, social media, blogs, grant writing — all gated by your permissions and human review before anything goes live.

    Time to value: 48 hours to first deploy. 30 days to first saved workday.

    Proof: NWKids — live, public, five years of documented work. EIN 46-4779591. Real land, real plants, real content, real people.

    Risk reversal: If the agent doesn't save you 10 hours in month one, we keep working until it does. Keep the system either way.

    Next step: One conversation. 15 minutes.

    Section 6

    Our Ask

    For Funders

    Microsoft AI for Good · Allen Institute · Google.org · Gates Foundation

    Fund the build. The grant covers a cohort of nonprofits who receive the system at no cost. In return: a verifiable, public case study proving AI agents can run nonprofit operations — the proof the entire sector is waiting for. GiveBIG and King County Best Starts for Kids are the local entry points.

    For Investors

    The Pauli Effect is a licensed social-purpose company with a defensible vertical, recurring revenue through the Knowledge API and management layer, and a live proof site in NWKids. We are seeking seed backing to deploy the first 20 client systems in the Pacific Northwest. The ownership model is the wedge; the Knowledge API is the compound.

    For Nonprofit Leaders

    You don't need to understand AI. You need an agent that understands your mission. Book 15 minutes. We'll show you the NWKids system running live, then tell you exactly what yours would do.

    The Pauli Effect

    Proudly Artificial. Built in Seattle. Serving Washington & Oregon.
    A licensed social-purpose company.

    NWKids — EIN 46-4779591 · Fiscally sponsored by Humanitarian Social Innovations.
    Your purchase supports our programs to keep food, water, energy, shelter — free, forever.

    Product Capability

    Living Documents

    We built micro apps we call Living Documents. They can be shared with teams and are specifically designed to be read by AI agents, have persistent memory, are easy to update, and can be a visual way to send offers, collect feedback, track projects, and collaborate with clients.

The site is under final construction and will turn five years of my experience here into a clear nonprofit story with program proposals, videos, photos, and proof metrics for visitors, potential clients, and funders.

    Indigo Azul aquaponic dome + culture kitchen concept
    Concept · Indigo Azul Systems Concept board · inspiration links only

    Indigo Azul — Aquaponic Dome & Culture Kitchen Concept

    This concept shows the next buildout vision for Indigo Azul: an aquaponic koi pond, geodesic dome, vertical grow towers, solar water circulation, and a culture-kitchen learning space that can host youth programs, interviews, funder visits, and community education. It was inspired by Farming Wonder, Zip Tie Domes, and the dome course I completed with Mr. Brown last year; once we have funding, the growing systems can be purchased from Lyine Group.

    ↗ Inspired by Farming Wonder ↗ Dome inspiration: Zip Tie Domes ↗ Mr. Brown course: DomeShells ↗ Systems to purchase once funded: Lyine Group

    Section 8

    Funding Alignment Outreach Board

    We designed these outreach boards to help ourselves and other clients in the future organize their campaigns. This board maps grant, corporate, university, philanthropic, and public-sector opportunities that align with New World Kids, The Pauli Effect, AI for Good, food security, youth programs, regenerative agriculture, digital equity, and community resilience.

    Each target can be marked as called, notes can be saved locally in the browser, and the red strike line makes completed outreach easy to scan during follow-up work.

    Grant calling list Click to open the full outreach board when you are ready to review or make calls. The rest of the document stays short and easy to scan. calling list

    Closing Note

    Final Thoughts

    Hopefully this wasn't too long and I didn't turn it into a podcast. If you find gaps I can fill them. For the next 90 days my main focus will mostly be to concentrate on this client and see what's repeatable and learn what it takes to deliver this type of offer consistently. I will also be following up on that call list to see what's possible. Seattle has always been a city of possibilities. I welcome your feedback when you get time. I'll be updating the Jetty app over the next few weeks with new features for you and creating a user guide. Hopefully it becomes something helpful over time. Take care out there and we will be in touch.

    Jeremy Bowers "J"

    New World Kids
